data.description:
These days it's easy to simply buy an index fund, and your returns should (roughly) match the market. But you can do a lot better than that by buying good quality businesses for attractive prices. For example, the Samchully Co.,Ltd (KRX:004690) share price is 85% higher than it was five years ago, which is more than the market average. It's fair to say the stock has continued its long term trend in the last year, over which it has risen 72%.

The past week has proven to be lucrative for SamchullyLtd investors, so let's see if fundamentals drove the company's five-year performance.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

In his essay The Superinvestors of Graham-and-Doddsville Warren Buffett described how share prices do not always rationally reflect the value of a business. One way to examine how market sentiment has changed over time is to look at the interaction between a company's share price and its earnings per share (EPS).

Over half a decade, SamchullyLtd managed to grow its earnings per share at 55% a year. The EPS growth is more impressive than the yearly share price gain of 13% over the same period. So one could conclude that the broader market has become more cautious towards the stock. This cautious sentiment is reflected in its (fairly low) P/E ratio of 5.32.

The image below shows how EPS has tracked over time (if you click on the image you can see greater detail).

This free interactive report on SamchullyLtd's earnings, revenue and cash flow is a great place to start, if you want to investigate the stock further.

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

When looking at investment returns, it is important to consider the difference between total shareholder return (TSR) and share price return. Whereas the share price return only reflects the change in the share price, the TSR includes the value of dividends (assuming they were reinvested) and the benefit of any discounted capital raising or spin-off. So for companies that pay a generous dividend, the TSR is often a lot higher than the share price return. We note that for SamchullyLtd the TSR over the last 5 years was 106%, which is better than the share price return mentioned above. And there's no prize for guessing that the dividend payments largely explain the divergence!

A Different Perspective

SamchullyLtd shareholders gained a total return of 78% during the year. Unfortunately this falls short of the market return. The silver lining is that the gain was actually better than the average annual return of 16% per year over five year. This suggests the company might be improving over time. While it is well worth considering the different impacts that market conditions can have on the share price, there are other factors that are even more important. To that end, you should be aware of the 1 warning sign we've spotted with SamchullyLtd .

data.title: We Think Oppstar Berhad (KLSE:OPPSTAR) Can Afford To Drive Business Growth
data.description:
Just because a business does not make any money, does not mean that the stock will go down. For example, although software-as-a-service business Salesforce.com lost money for years while it grew recurring revenue, if you held shares since 2005, you'd have done very well indeed. Having said that, unprofitable companies are risky because they could potentially burn through all their cash and become distressed.

So should Oppstar Berhad (KLSE:OPPSTAR) shareholders be worried about its cash burn? In this article, we define cash burn as its annual (negative) free cash flow, which is the amount of money a company spends each year to fund its growth. First, we'll determine its cash runway by comparing its cash burn with its cash reserves.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

When Might Oppstar Berhad Run Out Of Money?

A company's cash runway is calculated by dividing its cash hoard by its cash burn. In December 2025, Oppstar Berhad had RM110m in cash, and was debt-free. Importantly, its cash burn was RM3.2m over the trailing twelve months. So it had a very long cash runway of many years from December 2025. Even though this is but one measure of the company's cash burn, the thought of such a long cash runway warms our bellies in a comforting way. You can see how its cash balance has changed over time in the image below.

View our latest analysis for Oppstar Berhad

How Well Is Oppstar Berhad Growing?

We reckon the fact that Oppstar Berhad managed to shrink its cash burn by 37% over the last year is rather encouraging. But it makes us pessimistic to see that operating revenue slid 57% in that time. In light of the data above, we're fairly sanguine about the business growth trajectory. Clearly, however, the crucial factor is whether the company will grow its business going forward. So you might want to take a peek at how much the company is expected to grow in the next few years.

How Hard Would It Be For Oppstar Berhad To Raise More Cash For Growth?

Even though it seems like Oppstar Berhad is developing its business nicely, we still like to consider how easily it could raise more money to accelerate growth. Generally speaking, a listed business can raise new cash through issuing shares or taking on debt. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. By looking at a company's cash burn relative to its market capitalisation, we gain insight on how much shareholders would be diluted if the company needed to raise enough cash to cover another year's cash burn.

Oppstar Berhad's cash burn of RM3.2m is about 1.6% of its RM192m market capitalisation. So it could almost certainly just borrow a little to fund another year's growth, or else easily raise the cash by issuing a few shares.

So, Should We Worry About Oppstar Berhad's Cash Burn?

It may already be apparent to you that we're relatively comfortable with the way Oppstar Berhad is burning through its cash. In particular, we think its cash runway stands out as evidence that the company is well on top of its spending. While we must concede that its falling revenue is a bit worrying, the other factors mentioned in this article provide great comfort when it comes to the cash burn. Looking at all the measures in this article, together, we're not worried about its rate of cash burn; the company seems well on top of its medium-term spending needs. An in-depth examination of risks revealed 2 warning signs for Oppstar Berhad that readers should think about before committing capital to this stock.

data.title: Samjin LND (KOSDAQ:054090) Has Debt But No Earnings; Should You Worry?
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' When we think about how risky a company is, we always like to look at its use of debt, since debt overload can lead to ruin. We note that Samjin LND Co., Ltd. (KOSDAQ:054090) does have debt on its balance sheet. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

When Is Debt A Problem?

Debt assists a business until the business has trouble paying it off, either with new capital or with free cash flow. In the worst case scenario, a company can go bankrupt if it cannot pay its creditors. However, a more usual (but still expensive) situation is where a company must dilute shareholders at a cheap share price simply to get debt under control. By replacing dilution, though, debt can be an extremely good tool for businesses that need capital to invest in growth at high rates of return. When we think about a company's use of debt, we first look at cash and debt together.

What Is Samjin LND's Debt?

You can click the graphic below for the historical numbers, but it shows that Samjin LND had ₩60.4b of debt in September 2025, down from ₩67.3b, one year before. However, it does have ₩28.5b in cash offsetting this, leading to net debt of about ₩31.9b.

How Healthy Is Samjin LND's Balance Sheet?

According to the last reported balance sheet, Samjin LND had liabilities of ₩83.0b due within 12 months, and liabilities of ₩10.9b due beyond 12 months. Offsetting this, it had ₩28.5b in cash and ₩26.4b in receivables that were due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by ₩39.0b.

The deficiency here weighs heavily on the ₩16.3b company itself, as if a child were struggling under the weight of an enormous back-pack full of books, his sports gear, and a trumpet. So we'd watch its balance sheet closely, without a doubt. After all, Samjin LND would likely require a major re-capitalisation if it had to pay its creditors today. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. But it is Samjin LND's earnings that will influence how the balance sheet holds up in the future. So when considering debt, it's definitely worth looking at the earnings trend. Click here for an interactive snapshot.

Check out our latest analysis for Samjin LND

Over 12 months, Samjin LND made a loss at the EBIT level, and saw its revenue drop to ₩145b, which is a fall of 19%. That's not what we would hope to see.

Caveat Emptor

Not only did Samjin LND's revenue slip over the last twelve months, but it also produced negative earnings before interest and tax (EBIT). Its EBIT loss was a whopping ₩5.7b. When we look at that alongside the significant liabilities, we're not particularly confident about the company. We'd want to see some strong near-term improvements before getting too interested in the stock. For example, we would not want to see a repeat of last year's loss of ₩19b. And until that time we think this is a risky stock. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. data.title: These 4 Measures Indicate That Fuji SeikiLtd (TSE:6400) Is Using Debt Reasonably Well
data.description:
Legendary fund manager Li Lu (who Charlie Munger backed) once said, 'The biggest investment risk is not the volatility of prices, but whether you will suffer a permanent loss of capital.' It's only natural to consider a company's balance sheet when you examine how risky it is, since debt is often involved when a business collapses. Importantly, Fuji Seiki Co.,Ltd. (TSE:6400) does carry debt. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Is Debt Dangerous?

Debt and other liabilities become risky for a business when it cannot easily fulfill those obligations, either with free cash flow or by raising capital at an attractive price. If things get really bad, the lenders can take control of the business. However, a more common (but still painful) scenario is that it has to raise new equity capital at a low price, thus permanently diluting shareholders. Of course, debt can be an important tool in businesses, particularly capital heavy businesses. The first thing to do when considering how much debt a business uses is to look at its cash and debt together.

How Much Debt Does Fuji SeikiLtd Carry?

The image below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that at September 2025 Fuji SeikiLtd had debt of JP¥3.73b, up from JP¥3.33b in one year. However, it does have JP¥1.81b in cash offsetting this, leading to net debt of about JP¥1.92b.

How Healthy Is Fuji SeikiLtd's Balance Sheet?

According to the last reported balance sheet, Fuji SeikiLtd had liabilities of JP¥3.78b due within 12 months, and liabilities of JP¥2.26b due beyond 12 months. Offsetting these obligations, it had cash of JP¥1.81b as well as receivables valued at JP¥1.76b due within 12 months. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by JP¥2.48b.

Given this deficit is actually higher than the company's market capitalization of JP¥2.35b, we think shareholders really should watch Fuji SeikiLtd's debt levels, like a parent watching their child ride a bike for the first time. Hypothetically, extremely heavy dilution would be required if the company were forced to pay down its liabilities by raising capital at the current share price.

Check out our latest analysis for Fuji SeikiLtd

We measure a company's debt load relative to its earnings power by looking at its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and by calculating how easily its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) cover its interest expense (interest cover). The advantage of this approach is that we take into account both the absolute quantum of debt (with net debt to EBITDA) and the actual interest expenses associated with that debt (with its interest cover ratio).

We'd say that Fuji SeikiLtd's moderate net debt to EBITDA ratio ( being 1.7), indicates prudence when it comes to debt. And its strong interest cover of 12.2 times, makes us even more comfortable. Also good is that Fuji SeikiLtd grew its EBIT at 18% over the last year, further increasing its ability to manage debt. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. But you can't view debt in total isolation; since Fuji SeikiLtd will need earnings to service that debt. So if you're keen to discover more about its earnings, it might be worth checking out this graph of its long term earnings trend.

Finally, while the tax-man may adore accounting profits, lenders only accept cold hard cash. So it's worth checking how much of that EBIT is backed by free cash flow. During the last three years, Fuji SeikiLtd generated free cash flow amounting to a very robust 90% of its EBIT, more than we'd expect. That puts it in a very strong position to pay down debt.

Our View

The good news is that Fuji SeikiLtd's demonstrated ability to cover its interest expense with its EBIT delights us like a fluffy puppy does a toddler. But the stark truth is that we are concerned by its level of total liabilities. Looking at all the aforementioned factors together, it strikes us that Fuji SeikiLtd can handle its debt fairly comfortably. On the plus side, this leverage can boost shareholder returns, but the potential downside is more risk of loss, so it's worth monitoring the balance sheet. There's no doubt that we learn most about debt from the balance sheet. But ultimately, every company can contain risks that exist outside of the balance sheet. data.title: Does YMIRLINK,Inc. (TSE:4372) Create Value For Shareholders?
data.description:
One of the best investments we can make is in our own knowledge and skill set. With that in mind, this article will work through how we can use Return On Equity (ROE) to better understand a business. We'll use ROE to examine YMIRLINK,Inc. (TSE:4372), by way of a worked example.

Return on equity or ROE is a key measure used to assess how efficiently a company's management is utilizing the company's capital. In simpler terms, it measures the profitability of a company in relation to shareholder's equity.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

How Do You Calculate Return On Equity?

Return on equity can be calculated by using the formula:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for YMIRLINKInc is:

15% = JP¥451m ÷ JP¥2.9b (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' is the profit over the last twelve months. So, this means that for every ¥1 of its shareholder's investments, the company generates a profit of ¥0.15.

View our latest analysis for YMIRLINKInc

Does YMIRLINKInc Have A Good Return On Equity?

One simple way to determine if a company has a good return on equity is to compare it to the average for its industry. Importantly, this is far from a perfect measure, because companies differ significantly within the same industry classification. If you look at the image below, you can see YMIRLINKInc has a similar ROE to the average in the Software industry classification (14%).

That's neither particularly good, nor bad. While at least the ROE is not lower than the industry, its still worth checking what role the company's debt plays as high debt levels relative to equity may also make the ROE appear high. If true, then it is more an indication of risk than the potential.

The Importance Of Debt To Return On Equity

Most companies need money -- from somewhere -- to grow their profits. That cash can come from retained earnings, issuing new shares (equity), or debt. In the first and second cases, the ROE will reflect this use of cash for investment in the business. In the latter case, the use of debt will improve the returns, but will not change the equity. That will make the ROE look better than if no debt was used.

YMIRLINKInc's Debt And Its 15% ROE

One positive for shareholders is that YMIRLINKInc does not have any net debt! Its respectable ROE suggests it is a business worth watching, but it's even better the company achieved this without leverage. At the end of the day, when a company has zero debt, it is in a better position to take future growth opportunities.

Conclusion

Return on equity is useful for comparing the quality of different businesses. A company that can achieve a high return on equity without debt could be considered a high quality business. If two companies have around the same level of debt to equity, and one has a higher ROE, I'd generally prefer the one with higher ROE.

Having said that, while ROE is a useful indicator of business quality, you'll have to look at a whole range of factors to determine the right price to buy a stock. The rate at which profits are likely to grow, relative to the expectations of profit growth reflected in the current price, must be considered, too.   • Bears focus on that margin compression as a sign that hardware focused economics are under pressure, yet
    • they also assume in their own narrative that margins could reach 13.7% in about three years, which would be higher than the current 5.8% even though recent quarters include Q4 FY 2025 net income of just SEK3.7 million on SEK548.3 million of revenue, a relatively thin quarter.

TELUS (TSX:T) has put fresh numbers on the table for FY 2025, with third quarter revenue at CA$5.1b and basic EPS of CA$0.32 alongside net income of CA$493m as investors weigh the latest move in margins. The company has seen quarterly revenue move between CA$4.9b and CA$5.3b over the past six reported periods, while basic EPS has ranged from roughly CA$0.00 to CA$0.32. This sets a clear backdrop for how current earnings fit within its recent history. With net profit margins now sitting above last year, the focus for shareholders is increasingly on how sustainable that profitability profile looks from here.

See our full analysis for TELUS.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to line these results up against the dominant narratives around TELUS’s growth, risk and income profile to see which stories hold up and which start to look stretched.

See what the community is saying about TELUS

Advertisement

Net margin at 5.8% puts more weight on profitability quality

  • On a trailing basis TELUS reports a 5.8% net profit margin versus 4.6% a year earlier, and CA$1.2b of net income on CA$20.4b of revenue, so a bigger share of each dollar of sales is currently turning into profit.
  • What stands out against the bullish view is that, while bulls are looking for margins to reach 8.7%, the last twelve months are still anchored around 5.8%,
    • consensus of modest earnings growth of about 5.5% a year from this base supports the idea of steady progress but does not yet point to the step change in profitability that the most optimistic margin targets would imply,
    • and the earlier CA$591m one off loss in the trailing period means bulls need to separate that one time impact from the underlying margin trend when they judge how realistic those higher targets are.
On top of this set of numbers, some bulls argue the current profitability is just a starting point and see room for more improvement as TELUS leans into higher margin businesses like health and AI enabled services, as well as cost savings from digitisation and capital light partnerships. 🐂 TELUS Bull Case

P/E of 24.9x and 8.83% yield put pressure on cash coverage

  • The shares trade on a 24.9x P/E compared with 16.5x for the Global Telecom group and 9.2x for peers, while at the same time carrying an 8.83% dividend yield that is flagged as not being covered by either earnings or free cash flow.
  • Critics highlight that this combination challenges the bearish claim that the share price already reflects all the financial strain,
    • because weak interest coverage and a dividend that is not supported by current cash flows mean a lot of today’s return is coming from a payout that is stretching the balance sheet,
    • and with the share price at CA$18.96, any investor weighing the 8.83% income stream against those coverage issues has to think carefully about how much risk they are taking on for that level of yield.
Skeptics warn that if interest coverage and cash generation do not improve, the mix of a premium P/E, high dividend yield and elevated leverage could keep pressure on how the market values TELUS over time. 🐻 TELUS Bear Case

DCF fair value of CA$51.07 contrasts with 3.6% revenue growth forecast

  • The DCF fair value used in the analysis is CA$51.07 per share versus the current price of CA$18.96, while analysts are looking for revenue to grow about 3.6% a year and earnings about 5.5% a year from a trailing base of CA$20.4b of revenue and CA$1.2b of net income.
  • Consensus narrative notes that this large gap between the DCF fair value and the share price sits alongside only mid single digit forecast growth,
    • with earnings up 27.7% over the last year but down about 10% a year over five years, so the recent improvement still sits against a weaker multi year trend,
    • and that mix of modest growth forecasts, a premium 24.9x P/E and the CA$51.07 DCF fair value means investors need to decide whether the current price is simply cautious or whether it reflects ongoing concerns about leverage and the sustainability of the 8.83% dividend.

Why Viscofan’s latest buyback may matter for shareholders

Viscofan (BME:VIS) recently repurchased 43,228 shares under its ongoing buyback programme. This move highlights active capital management and could slowly reshape the company’s shareholder base.

This kind of regulated buyback activity can influence metrics such as earnings per share over time. It can also offer investors a clearer view of how management thinks about Viscofan’s financial position and long term value creation.

See our latest analysis for Viscofan.

At a share price of €57.3, Viscofan has had a 90 day share price return of 8.32% and a 5 year total shareholder return of 17.29%.

If Viscofan’s recent buyback has you thinking about ownership and long term alignment, it might be a good time to broaden your search and check out 100 top founder-led companies.

With Viscofan trading at €57.3 and sitting at a discount to analyst targets and certain intrinsic estimates, the real question for you is whether this is a genuine value opportunity or if the market is already pricing in future growth.

Price-to-Earnings of 16.4x: Is it justified?

On a P/E of 16.4x at a share price of €57.3, Viscofan sits slightly below both analyst targets and some intrinsic estimates, while still pricing in ongoing profitability.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price to earnings per share, so it effectively tells you how much investors are paying today for each euro of current earnings. For a profitable food sector company like Viscofan, this is a commonly used yardstick because earnings are a core driver of long term returns.

Viscofan is described as good value versus both the European Food industry average P/E of 17.3x and a peer average of 26.8x, suggesting the market is not assigning a premium price to its earnings. At the same time, the estimated fair P/E is 16.2x, slightly below the current 16.4x level, which implies the ratio is close to a level the market could move towards if sentiment or expectations shift.

Compared with the broader European Food group at 17.3x and peers at 26.8x, Viscofan’s 16.4x multiple looks restrained rather than stretched, even though it sits just above the internally estimated fair ratio of 16.2x. That mix of a modest discount to sector averages and a small premium to fair ratio gives investors a tight reference band for judging whether the current price feels comfortable.

data.title: Entra (OB:ENTRA) One Off Gain Lifts Profitability And Complicates Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Entra (OB:ENTRA) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of NOK 780 million and basic EPS of NOK 1.89, rounding out a year in which quarterly revenue stayed in a tight NOK 762 million to NOK 780 million band and EPS ranged from NOK 1.02 to NOK 2.18. The company has seen revenue move from NOK 756 million in Q4 2024 to NOK 780 million in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from NOK 3.18 in Q4 2024 to NOK 1.89 in Q4 2025. This sets up a year where investors are likely to focus on how underlying margins are holding up across the portfolio.

See our full analysis for Entra.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the dominant narratives around Entra, and where the figures start to challenge those storylines.

See what the community is saying about Entra

Advertisement

TTM profitability helped by NOK 305m one off

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Entra earned NOK 1.2b of net income excluding extra items on NOK 3.1b of revenue, and that figure includes a NOK 305m one off gain that lifted the result.
  • Bulls highlight that Entra has moved from losses a year ago to profit, which lines up with the data, but the one off gain means:
    • TTM EPS of NOK 6.33 compares with very weak EPS figures in 2024 (including a period with EPS of NOK 19.36 loss). The swing into profit is therefore partly mechanical rather than purely from recurring operations.
    • Forecast earnings growth of about 6.4% a year is being judged on this uplifted base. Investors need to separate the NOK 305m boost from the underlying NOK 1.2b of net income when thinking about how durable that growth might be.
Entra’s recent move back into profit is exactly what optimists have been waiting for, but bulls themselves acknowledge that a NOK 305m boost makes it harder to tell how much of the turnaround is repeatable, which is what really matters over the next few years. 🐂 Entra Bull Case

Interest coverage flagged as major weak spot

  • The risk summary calls out that earnings in the last 12 months do not comfortably cover interest expenses, even with NOK 1.2b of net income and NOK 3.1b of revenue on a trailing basis.
  • Bears focus on this weak interest coverage as a core concern, and the recent numbers give them some support:
    • Five year earnings have fallen at about 44.5% a year, so the current profit level sits against a backdrop of long term earnings pressure that can make servicing debt more sensitive to any setback.
    • With forecasts pointing to only about 2.5% yearly revenue growth, any rise in funding costs or slower rental growth would leave less room for interest to be paid comfortably out of ongoing income.
Skeptics point out that a capital intensive property owner with weak interest coverage is exposed if funding stays demanding, and the combination of modest forecast revenue growth and a long term earnings decline trend gives that argument real bite. 🐻 Entra Bear Case

P/E around 18x against DCF fair value of NOK 4.19

  • Entra trades on a trailing P/E of about 18x at a share price of NOK 113.60, close to peer levels, while a DCF fair value in the data sits far lower at NOK 4.19.
  • Consensus narrative leans on recurring cash flows from Norwegian offices, and the tension between these valuation markers is clear:
    • The 18x P/E is in line with direct peers but higher than the 14x average for the wider European real estate sector, suggesting the market is willing to pay a premium for Entra’s current earnings stream.
    • The DCF fair value of NOK 4.19, far below both the NOK 113.60 share price and an analyst target of NOK 114.00, reflects a much more cautious view of long term cash flows than the multiple based pricing implies.

data.title: Founder Returns As GEO Group Activates US$520m Contracts And Seeks Value
data.description:
• GEO Group (NYSE:GEO) has announced a major CEO transition as founder Dr. George C. Zoley returns to lead the company.
  • The change follows what the company describes as record new business wins and the largest facility expansion in its history.
  • New or expanded contracts are tied to approximately $520 million in annualized revenue and the activation of five major facilities.

GEO Group operates correctional, detention, and community reentry facilities, so leadership changes can matter for how contracts are pursued, managed, and renewed. The announcement comes alongside significant operational growth, with multiple new or expanded agreements and several large facilities being activated. These developments can shape how investors view NYSE:GEO as a going concern. For readers tracking contract-driven business models, the combination of fresh capacity and a returning founder is a key development to watch.

For investors, the main question now is how Dr. Zoley’s return could influence priorities such as capital allocation, contract bidding, and balance sheet discipline. As the company works through the integration and activation of approximately $520 million in annualized contract value, attention will likely focus on execution quality, cost control, and any commentary on future growth initiatives or risk management.
